revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Bettoji Temple]][[ja:別当寺]]
{{Unreferenced|date=October 2019|qq=}} '''Bettōji''' (別当寺) refers to temples established to manage {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrines|1=Shinto shrine}} prior to the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, during the period of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|en|Shinbutsu-shūgō|qid=Q2279088|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}} (the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism). These temples conducted shrine rituals in a Buddhist style, including sutra chanting before the deity. The head priest responsible for these duties was called a '''{{ill|Bettō|en|Bettō|qid=Q2900274|lt=Bettō|1=Bettō}}''' (chief of shrine monks). As such, temples housing these leaders were referred to as Bettōji. They were also known by terms such as '''{{ill|Jingū-ji|en|Jingū-ji|qid=Q1074971|lt=Jingū-ji|1=Jingū-ji}}''', '''Jingōji''' (神護寺), or '''Miyadera''' (宮寺). == Overview == The term "Bettō" (別当) literally means "one who assumes a separate duty" and originally referred to a position where someone managed temple affairs as an additional responsibility. Bettōji emerged during the era of [[Honji suijaku]], a belief system identifying shrine deities as manifestations (suijaku) of Buddhist figures. This concept integrated shrines and temples, often resulting in temples (Bettōji) being established within shrine precincts. Until the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|qid=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration|1=Meiji Restoration}}, the Bettō wielded significant authority over shrine administration, often outRanking the {{ill|Guji|ja|宮司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Guji|1=Gūji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11453500}} (Shinto chief priest). The prominence of Bettōji can be attributed to historical circumstances, such as the lack of a centralized family registration system in early Japan. Temples often served as de facto registrars, kept ledgers equivalent to census records, and managed large landholdings. They even issued travel permits, underscoring their influence. A Bettōji would often oversee multiple shrines within a village, consolidating the religious and administrative functions of a community under one institution. In Shinto, deities are not represented as idols but through objects such as the {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|en|Imperial Regalia of Japan|qid=Q327465|lt=Three Sacred Treasures|1=Imperial Regalia of Japan}} or natural objects like sacred mountains. Bettōji facilitated the worship of shrine deities as Buddhist figures (honji-butsu), allowing followers to revere both Shinto kami and Buddhist manifestations harmoniously. Although the presence of a Bettō suggested Buddhist practices, it did not exclude Shinto rituals. Guji conducted Shinto ceremonies, while Bettō performed Buddhist services, creating a dual-religious practice among worshipers. This system exemplified the blended religious landscape of pre-Meiji Japan. The {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|qid=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu Bunri|1=Shinbutsu bunri}} decree of the Meiji era ended this practice, leading to the separation of Shinto and Buddhism and the dissolution of Bettōji. == Notes == {{Citation help|qq=}} {{Reflist|qq=}} {{Buddhism-stub|qq=}} {{Shinto-stub|qq=}}
{{translated page|ja|別当寺|version=91512915|comment=Imported full ja history}}
